nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and still freshwaters; floats on the surface of ponds, ditches, marshes and slow streams, often forming dense mats among other floating vegetation

decorPreferences: open water surface for floating mats
stones or driftwood with fishing line or mesh for tied carpets
moderate flow only — strong flow breaks up mats

goodWith: labyrinth fish that use floating cover
avoidWith: tanks with strong surface agitation that disperses mats

summary: Floating Crystalwort (Riccia fluitans) is a cosmopolitan aquatic liverwort with bright-green forked thalli. Commonly kept floating as a fry refuge, or tied down to stone with fishing line as a carpet — a technique popularized by Takashi Amano.
careNotes: Riccia fluitans is a floating liverwort (division Marchantiophyta), not a true vascular plant — it has no functional roots and absorbs nutrients directly through its thallus surface. It can be grown two ways with very different husbandry profiles. (1) Floating: the natural mode. Forms dense bright-green mats at the surface, copes with low to medium light, needs no CO2, and serves as excellent cover for fry and shrimplets. (2) Tied carpet: branches are tied to stones or pieces of driftwood (with fine fishing line, cotton thread, or stainless steel mesh) and placed on the substrate. This technique was popularized by Takashi Amano in the early 1990s and produces a striking foreground carpet that pearls visibly under good CO2 — but new shoots always grow toward the surface, so the carpet tends to detach and float away over time, particularly under higher light and CO2. Regular pruning back to short stubble is needed to keep a tied carpet anchored; expect ongoing re-tying. For the carpet mode, sources generally agree on medium light (around 0.5 W/L), 6–14 mg/L CO2, and patient grooming. Substrate type is largely irrelevant since the plant has no roots. Temperature tolerance is broad (15–30 °C survivable per Aqua-fish.net) but optimal in the 20–26 °C range; the 20–28 °C range here reflects the typical community-tank operating window. pH 6.0–8.0 from Buce Plant and Tropica. Cross-source disagreement: Tropica calls difficulty 'medium' (due to the carpet-detachment problem), while Buce Plant and FishLab call overall care 'easy' — 'intermediate' adopted as the average, with the caveat that floating use is genuinely beginner-friendly and the carpet mode is what raises the difficulty. Growth rate is generally reported as medium; Aqua-fish.net describes faster growth (3–5 cm every two months) under good light and CO2. Watch for hair algae, which easily overtakes Riccia mats, and for duckweed contamination, which can outcompete it on the surface.
breedingNotes: Propagation is straightforward by fragmentation: simply break the thallus into smaller pieces and re-tie or re-float them. Riccia fluitans also releases spores from its branch tips that drift away and establish new colonies on their own. Tissue-culture cups are commonly available and arrive pest- and algae-free; rinse the gel away before introducing to the tank.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Primary source for height (1–5 cm), light (medium, ~0.5 W/L), CO2 recommendation (6–14 mg/L), growth rate (medium), origin (cosmopolitan), and the Amano-popularized tied-carpet technique.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked light (low to medium), CO2 (not necessary but recommended), pH (6–8), and both placement modes (free-floating mat vs attached to hardscape/mesh). Confirms tied carpets tend to grow upward and detach without pruning.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (optimal 20–26 °C, tolerated 15–30 °C), pH (6.0–8.0), placement versatility, and Takashi Amano's 1990s popularization of submerged use.

additional.name: Aqua-fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked growth rate (3–5 cm per two months), light (moderate to high depending on placement), CO2 (6–14 mg/L for submerged carpet), and care level (easy in floating mode).

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Taxonomy source: family Ricciaceae, order Marchantiales, division Marchantiophyta (liverworts). Confirms cosmopolitan distribution, pond habitat, and competition pressure from duckweed and hair algae.
